longDescription:
Eastern Turkey (visible from Armenia) Mount Ararat (Turkish: Agri Dagi, Armenian: Masis) is a snow-capped dormant volcano and the highest
                peak in Turkey, rising 5,137 meters (16,854 feet) near the border with Armenia and Iran. For thousands
                of years, this majestic twin-peaked mountain has been identified as the resting place of Noah's Ark
                after the Great Flood - the place where humanity began anew. While the Bible speaks only of the
                "mountains of Ararat" (a region, not a specific peak), tradition has fixed this particular mountain
                as the site where Noah, his family, and the animals disembarked to repopulate the earth. This
                identification has made Ararat one of the most symbolically powerful mountains in the Abrahamic traditions.

name: Mount Ararat
description:
Explore temples, mountains, groves, and holy sites that have served as gateways between the human and divine realms
                across cultures and millennia. From ancient stone circles to living pilgrimage destinations, discover the places
                where heaven touches earth.
significance:
Eastern Turkey (visible from Armenia) Mount Ararat (Turkish: Agri Dagi, Armenian: Masis) is a snow-capped dormant volcano and the highest
                peak in Turkey, rising 5,137 meters (16,854 feet) near the border with Armenia and Iran. For thousands
                of years, this majestic twin-peaked mountain has been identified as the resting place of Noah's Ark
                after the Great Flood - the place where humanity began anew.

geography: Elevated terrain with commanding views; often snow-capped or shrouded in mist, symbolizing proximity to the divine

keyMyths.title: Noah's Ark Landing
keyMyths.description: According to the Book of Genesis, after the Great Flood, Noah's Ark came to rest on the mountains of Ararat. This event marks the repopulation of the earth and God's covenant with humanity.

keyMyths.title: The Search for the Ark
keyMyths.description: Throughout history, numerous expeditions have been launched to find the physical remains of Noah's Ark on Mount Ararat. These searches are driven by religious faith and the desire for archaeological confirmation of the biblical narrative.
keyMyths.source: Various accounts of expeditions, e.g., Fernand Navarra's expedition (claimed finding wooden remains).

keyMyths.title: Symbol of Hope and Renewal
keyMyths.description: Mount Ararat, as the landing site of the Ark, symbolizes hope and the renewal of life after devastation. It represents the promise of God's mercy and the opportunity for humanity to rebuild.
keyMyths.source: Interpretations of Genesis narrative in theological commentaries.

keyMyths.title: National Symbol of Armenia
keyMyths.description: Despite being located in Turkey, Mount Ararat holds immense cultural and national significance for Armenians. It is a prominent symbol of Armenian identity and heritage, often depicted in art and literature.
keyMyths.source: Armenian national identity and cultural texts.

keyMyths.title: Challenges to the Ark Narrative
keyMyths.description: Geological and scientific evidence presents challenges to the literal interpretation of the Noah's Ark story. Alternative interpretations focus on the symbolic meaning of the flood narrative and its moral lessons.
keyMyths.source: Geological and archaeological studies of the Ararat region; theological debates on biblical literalism.

sources.name: The Book of Genesis
sources.description: The primary source for the Noah's Ark narrative.
sources.title: The Book of Genesis

sources.name: Josephus, Flavius. *Antiquities of the Jews*.
sources.description: First-century historian who recounts the story of Noah's Ark and mentions the mountains of Ararat.
sources.title: Josephus, Flavius. *Antiquities of the Jews*.

sources.name: Whitcomb, John C., and Henry M. Morris. *The Genesis Flood: The Biblical Record and Its Scientific Implications*.
sources.description: A prominent work in creation science that argues for a literal interpretation of the Genesis flood narrative.
sources.title: Whitcomb, John C., and Henry M. Morris. *The Genesis Flood: The Biblical Record and Its Scientific Implications*.

sources.name: Montgomery, John Warwick. *The Quest for Noah's Ark*.
sources.description: A critical examination of the various expeditions and claims related to the search for Noah's Ark.
sources.title: Montgomery, John Warwick. *The Quest for Noah's Ark*.

sources.name: Allen, W. Sidney. *Living Armenian*.
sources.description: A study of Armenian culture, with mentions of Mount Ararat's importance.
sources.title: Allen, W. Sidney. *Living Armenian*.
